Creating the bayesdir R package 1 Introduction

Creating the bayesdir R package Sheng Jiang 2026-05-29 1 Introduction This package implements bayesdir, Bayesian directional data analysis, including model 1, model 2. The documentation and package are both created using one simple command: litr::render("index.Rmd",output_format = litr_gitbook(minimal_eval = TRUE)) ## install.packages("./bayesdir/", repos = NULL, type="source") This line is purely for testing (to be deleted later!). # Complete development workflow my_load <- function(){ litr::render("index.Rmd", output_format = litr::litr_gitbook(minimal_eval = TRUE)) devtools::document("./bayesdir") # Update documentation devtools::load_all("./bayesdir") } my_load() # Step 2: Check the package (optional but recommended) devtools::check("./bayesdir", document = FALSE) # Step 3: Install the package locally for full installation devtools::install("./bayesdir") # Step 4: Load the installed package (optional, if you want to test installation) library(bayesdir) setwd("~/repos/bayesdir/") ## 1. Delete the auto-generated _main.Rmd file unlink("_main.Rmd") unlink("_bookdown_files", recursive = TRUE) if(!dir.create("bayesdir")){ unlink("bayesdir", recursive = TRUE) } ## 2. Re-run the render command litr::render( "index.Rmd", output_format = litr::litr_gitbook(minimal_eval = TRUE) ) ## 3. Document and load the package devtools::document("bayesdir") devtools::load_all("bayesdir")

2 Package setup The DESCRIPTION file is created using this code. usethis::create_package( path = ".", fields = list( Package = params$package_name, Version = "0.0.0.9000", Title = "bayesdir", Description = "Implements Bayesian directional data analysis for von Mises-Fisher models and related methods.", `Authors@R` = person( given = "Sheng", family = "Jiang", email = "jiangsheng@cuhk.edu.cn", role = c("aut", "cre") ) ) ) usethis::use_mit_license(copyright_holder = "Sheng Jiang") The following is what will show up when someone types package?bayesdir in the console. #' bayesdir #' #' This package implements the `bayesdir` method. #' #' @docType package This package will have some dependancies: ##library(ggplot2) ##usethis::use_package("tidyverse", type = "depends") ## usethis::use_package("ggplot2") usethis::use_package("ggplot2", type = "imports") usethis::use_package("MASS", type = "imports") usethis::use_package("parallel", type = "imports") usethis::use_package("pbmcapply",type = "imports") usethis::use_package("stats", type = "imports") usethis::use_package("rstiefel", type = "imports") usethis::use_package("tidyverse", type = "imports") usethis::use_pipe(export=TRUE) #' @importFrom parallel mclapply #' @importFrom stats quantile rnorm sd uniroot #' @importFrom rstiefel rmf.vector #' @importFrom pbmcapply pbmclapply #' @importFrom magrittr %>% NULL
3 Helpers functions 3.1 Summarize functions 3.2 Plot Functions

3 Helpers functions These are some helper functions that are needed for the posterior computation These functions are not documented for now. (TODO: do this). #' Compute the function A(kappa) = I_{p/2}(kappa) / I_{p/2-1}(kappa) #' for the von Mises–Fisher distribution. #' @param kappa concentration parameter (non‑negative scalar) #' @param p space dimension (default = 2) #' @return scalar value of A(kappa) #' @export get_Ap <- function(kappa, p = 2) { if (kappa < 1e-6) return(0) besselI(kappa, p/2, expon.scaled = TRUE) / besselI(kappa, p/2 - 1, expon.scaled = TRUE) } #' Compute the score function s(eta, x) for vMF. #' @param eta natural parameter vector (length p) #' @param x unit vector observation (length p) #' @param p space dimension (default = 2) #' @return score vector of length p #' @export score_fn <- function(eta, x, p = 2) { kappa <- sqrt(sum(eta^2)) if (kappa < 1e-6) return(x) expected <- get_Ap(kappa, p) * eta / kappa x - expected } #' Fisher information matrix for the vMF model #' #' @param C natural parameter vector (length p) #' @param p dimension (defaults to length(C)) #' @return p x p Fisher information matrix #' @export get_FIM_vmf <- function(C, p = length(C)) { kappa <- sqrt(sum(C^2)) I_p <- diag(p) if (kappa < 1e-6) return(I_p / p) mu <- C / kappa Ap <- get_Ap(kappa, p) Ap_prime <- 1 - Ap^2 - ((p - 1) / kappa) * Ap mu_mu <- mu %*% t(mu) Ap_prime * mu_mu + (Ap / kappa) * (I_p - mu_mu) } #' Compute the sum of weights sum_{t = start}^{end} 1/(t + N)^2. #' @param n number of observed data points #' @param M number of iterations #' @export compute_weights <- function(n, M) { t_early <- 1:M W_nm <- sum(1 / (n + t_early)^2) # Infinite sum R_nm = sum_{t=M+1}^\infty 1/(n+t)^2 approx 1/(n+M) R_nm <- 1 / (n + M) list(W_nm = W_nm, R_nm = R_nm) } #' compute inverse for squared matrix #' @param M matrix #' @param tol tolerence erro #' @return M^{-1} #' @export safe_sym_inverse <- function(M, tol = 1e-10) { eig <- eigen((M + t(M)) / 2, symmetric = TRUE) vals_inv <- 1 / pmax(eig$values, tol) eig$vectors %*% diag(vals_inv) %*% t(eig$vectors) } #'generate n vmf data when p=2 #' @param n number of observations #' @param mu_true,kappa_true true parameter #' @return list containing both cartesian and polara coodinates type. #' @export generate_vmf_data <- function(n, mu_true, kappa_true) { eta_true <- kappa_true * c(cos(mu_true), sin(mu_true)) raw_samples <- replicate(n, rstiefel::rmf.vector(eta_true))[,1,] X_cartesian <- matrix(raw_samples, nrow = 2, ncol = n) theta_rad <- atan2(X_cartesian[2, ], X_cartesian[1, ]) list(X = X_cartesian, theta = theta_rad) } #' Compute MLE given data X #' @param X nrow = p ncol = number of observation #' @return MLE as a p-dim vector #' @export mle_vmf <- function(X){ n <- ncol(X) sum_vec <- rowSums(X) # 2-dim vector sum Robs <- sqrt(sum(sum_vec^2)) Rbar <- Robs / n mu_hat <- atan2(sum_vec[2], sum_vec[1]) if (Rbar < 0.001) { kappa_hat <- 0 } else if (Rbar > 0.999) { kappa_hat <- 1 / (2 * (1 - Rbar)) } else { kappa_hat <- stats::uniroot(function(k) get_Ap(k, 2) - Rbar, c(0.001, 100))$root } c_hat <- kappa_hat * c(cos(mu_hat), sin(mu_hat)) return(c_hat) } 3.1 Summarize functions #' Helper to compute statistics (Coverage, Length, Variance) #' @param samples samples of parameters on the real line #' @param true_val ground truth on real line #' @return list contain stats e,g, cover or not, len of confidence interval, variance. #' @export get_stats <- function(samples, true_val) { ci <- quantile(samples, probs = c(0.025, 0.975)) list( cover = as.numeric(true_val >= ci[1] && true_val <= ci[2]), len = as.numeric(ci[2] - ci[1]), est_var = var(samples) ) } #' Final Summarization #' @param rel_list a list containing all samples from all simulations #' @param method_name for example, "MCMC". #' @return list containing all statistics #' @export summarize_df <- function(res_list, method_name) { df <- do.call(rbind, res_list) data.frame( Method = method_name, Cov_mu = mean(df$c_mu) * 100, Len_mu = mean(df$l_mu), Cov_ka = mean(df$c_ka) * 100, Len_ka = mean(df$l_ka), Time = mean(df$time) ) } 3.2 Plot Functions #' Plot Trace Lines with Sideways Density #' @param paths A matrix where each row represents a chain's trajectory and each column represents an iteration. #' If NULL, only the density plot is shown. #' @param samples A numeric vector of the final posterior samples used to compute the density. #' @param iter A numeric vector for the X-axis iterations. If NULL, defaults to 0:(ncol(paths)-1). #' @param ref_value A numeric value for a horizontal reference line (e.g., the true parameter value). #' @param ref_density_fun A function that takes a numeric vector and returns density values (e.g., an MCMC benchmark). #' @param xlab X-axis label. Defaults to "Forward step i" for traces or "Density" for pure density plots. #' @param ylab Y-axis label. Defaults to expression(mu). #' @param main Plot title. #' @param trace_col Color for the trajectory lines. Usually includes transparency. #' @param density_fill Fill color for the sideways density polygon. #' @param density_border Border color for the density polygon. #' @param ref_density_col Color for the reference density curve. #' @param n_value Optional sample size value (currently not used in plot logic). #' @param show_right_axis Logical; if TRUE, displays the Y-axis on the right side as well. #' @param xlim_multiplier Factor to extend the X-axis to accommodate the sideways density. #' @param y_range Manual limits for the Y-axis. If NULL, calculated automatically. #' @param mark_initial Logical; if TRUE, marks the starting point of the first chain. #' @param initial_col Color for the initial point marker. #' @param initial_cex Character expansion (size) for the initial point marker. #' #' @return No return value, called for side effects (plotting). #' @export plot_trace_sideways_density <- function( paths = NULL, samples, iter = NULL, ref_value = NULL, ref_density_fun = NULL, xlab = NULL, ylab = expression(mu), main = NULL, trace_col = rgb(0.25, 0.45, 0.75, 0.06), density_fill = rgb(0.25, 0.45, 0.75, 0.30), density_border = rgb(0.20, 0.35, 0.65), ref_density_col = "red", n_value = NULL, show_right_axis = FALSE, xlim_multiplier = 1.25, y_range = NULL, mark_initial = TRUE, initial_col = "red", initial_cex = 1.2 ) { # --- 1. Data Handling: Securely process paths and iterations --- if (!is.null(paths)) { if (!is.matrix(paths)) paths <- as.matrix(paths) if (is.null(iter)) iter <- seq_len(ncol(paths)) - 1 x_start <- min(iter) x_end <- max(iter) x_width <- x_end - x_start # Offset where the density plot begins (just to the right of the traces) x_offset <- x_start + x_width * 1.02 current_xlab <- ifelse(is.null(xlab), "Forward step i", xlab) } else { # Pure density mode: set up dummy X-axis ranges x_start <- 0 x_end <- 0.05 x_width <- 1 x_offset <- 0 current_xlab <- ifelse(is.null(xlab), "Density", xlab) } # Define Y-axis limits if (is.null(y_range)) { y_range <- range(c(if(!is.null(paths)) as.vector(paths), samples, ref_value), finite = TRUE) y_pad <- 0.1 * diff(y_range) y_range <- y_range + c(-y_pad, y_pad) } # --- 2. Density Calculation --- dens <- density(samples, n = 512) y_grid <- seq(y_range[1], y_range[2], length.out = 500) ref_dens_vals <- if(!is.null(ref_density_fun)) ref_density_fun(y_grid) else NULL # Scaling: Density takes 25% of width if traces exist, otherwise 90% max_d <- max(c(dens$y, ref_dens_vals), na.rm = TRUE) dens_scale <- if(!is.null(paths)) (x_width * 0.25) / max_d else (x_width * 0.9) / max_d x_max <- if(!is.null(paths)) x_start + x_width * xlim_multiplier else x_width # --- 3. Plot Initialization --- plot(NULL, xlim = c(x_start, x_max), ylim = y_range, xlab = "", ylab = ylab, main = main, xaxt = "n", yaxt = "n", las = 1) # Add Left Y-axis axis(2, las = 1) # Add Right Y-axis if requested if (show_right_axis) axis(4, las = 1) # --- 4. Draw X-axis Labels --- if (!is.null(paths)) { ticks <- pretty(c(x_start, x_end)) axis(1, at = ticks, labels = ticks) mtext(current_xlab, side = 1, line = 2.5) } else { d_ticks <- pretty(c(0, max_d)) axis(1, at = x_offset + d_ticks * dens_scale, labels = d_ticks) mtext(current_xlab, side = 1, line = 2.5) } # --- 5. Plot Trajectories --- if (!is.null(paths)) { for (i in seq_len(nrow(paths))) { lines(iter, paths[i, ], col = trace_col, lwd = 0.5) } # Optionally mark the starting point (e.g., MLE or initial guess) if (mark_initial) { points(iter[1], paths[1, 1], col = initial_col, pch = 19, cex = initial_cex) } } # --- 6. Plot Densities (Sideways) --- # Draw the main density polygon polygon(x_offset + dens$y * dens_scale, dens$x, col = density_fill, border = density_border, lwd = 1.2) # Draw the reference density line if provided if (!is.null(ref_dens_vals)) { lines(x_offset + ref_dens_vals * dens_scale, y_grid, col = ref_density_col, lwd = 2) } # Add reference horizontal line (True Value) if (!is.null(ref_value)) abline(h = ref_value, col = "red", lty = 2) # Vertical separator line between trace and density segments(x_offset, y_range[1], x_offset, y_range[2], col = "gray60", lty = 3) }

5 Predictive Resampler 5.1 The main sampler The main Gibbs sampler is called run_predresamp_vmf(). #' Simulate Raw Score Path (Step 1 of Algorithm 1) #' @param c0 Initial estimator (MLE) #' @param n Initial sample size #' @param M Depth (iterations) #' @param save_raw Whether to save the trajectory of c_n,m #' @return A list containing the raw difference D, quadratic variation Q, and optional path #' @export simulate_raw_path <- function(c0, n, M, save_raw = FALSE) { p <- length(c0) c_curr <- c0 Q_mat <- matrix(0, p, p) if (save_raw) { c_chain <- matrix(0, p, M + 1) c_chain[, 1] <- c0 } for (m in 1:M) { gamma_m <- 1 / (n + m) # 1. Draw X_new ~ vMF(c_curr) x_new <- rstiefel::rmf.vector(c_curr) # 2. Compute score s s <- score_fn(c_curr, x_new, p) # 3. Accumulate quadratic variation Q Q_mat <- Q_mat + (gamma_m^2) * (s %*% t(s)) # 4. Raw score update c_curr <- c_curr + gamma_m * s if (save_raw) { c_chain[, m + 1] <- c_curr } } res <- list( D_raw = c_curr - c0, # Raw difference (c_n,M - c_n,0) Q_nM = Q_mat, c_final_raw = c_curr ) if (save_raw) res$c_chain <- c_chain return(res) } #' Score-Only Martingale Posterior Sampler for vMF #' @param X Data matrix (p x n) #' @param B Number of chains #' @param M Depth M #' @param type "RS-PC" or "HRS-PC" #' @param cal "pooled" or "pathwise" #' @param n_cores Number of cores #' @param save_raw Whether to return the raw score paths #' @export run_predresamp_vmf <- function(X, B = 5000, M = 50, type = c("HRS-PC", "RS-PC"), cal = c("pooled", "pathwise"), n_cores = 1, save_raw = FALSE) { type <- match.arg(type) cal <- match.arg(cal) n <- ncol(X) p <- nrow(X) # --- Step 0: Global Setup --- c0 <- mle_vmf(X) weights <- compute_weights(n, M) # Should return w_nM and r_nM w_nM <- weights$W_nm r_nM <- weights$R_nm # --- Step 1: Simulate Raw Score Paths (Parallel) --- raw_results <- parallel::mclapply(1:B, function(b) { simulate_raw_path(c0, n, M, save_raw = save_raw) }, mc.cores = n_cores) # --- Step 2: Construct Calibration Estimator --- if (cal == "pooled") { Q_list <- lapply(raw_results, `[[`, "Q_nM") bar_Q <- Reduce("+", Q_list) / B I_pool <- bar_Q / w_nM } # --- Step 3: Return Posterior Draws --- # Define Post-correction helper C_epsilon correct_fn <- function(diff_vec, I_mat) { c0 + safe_sym_inverse(I_mat) %*% diff_vec } posterior_samples <- sapply(1:B, function(b) { # Get I estimator for this chain I_b <- if (cal == "pathwise") raw_results[[b]]$Q_nM / w_nM else I_pool D_b <- raw_results[[b]]$D_raw if (type == "RS-PC") { # Standard correction: c_out = C(c_nM) c_out <- correct_fn(D_b, I_b) } else { # Hybrid correction: c_out = C(c_nM + Z) # Draw Gaussian Tail Z ~ N(0, r_nM * I_b) tail_cov <- r_nM * I_b # Matrix sqrt for sampling e <- eigen(tail_cov) Z_b <- (e$vectors %*% diag(sqrt(pmax(e$values, 0)), p) %*% t(e$vectors)) %*% rnorm(p) c_out <- correct_fn(D_b + Z_b, I_b) } return(c_out) }) # Transform to Polar for consistency with your previous code kappa_sam <- apply(posterior_samples, 2, function(v) sqrt(sum(v^2))) if (p == 2) { mu_sam <- apply(posterior_samples, 2, function(v) { res <- atan2(v[2], v[1]) if (res < 0) res <- res + 2 * pi return(res) }) } else { mu_sam <- sweep(posterior_samples, 2, kappa_sam, "/") } out <- list( samples = list( c_sam = posterior_samples, mu_sam = mu_sam, kappa_sam = kappa_sam ), setup = list(type = type, cal = cal, I_pool = if(cal=="pooled") I_pool else NULL) ) # If requested, save the raw paths from Step 1 if (save_raw) { out$raw_chains <- lapply(raw_results, `[[`, "c_chain") } return(out) }

6 Simulation Study: Bayesian vMF models for current direction Data with p=2 This vignette demonstrates a complete simulation study using bayesdir on synthetic traffic data from a grid road network. 6.1 Sythetic data for illustration with p=2 Generate data from von-mises fisher distribution. return in both cartesian coordinate and polar coordinate. Here \(\mu \in [0,2\pi]\) as an angle. \(\kappa \geq0\) as concentration. An example, For p >2, simply follow \(\texttt{rstiefel::rmf.vector}\), below we have an example. 6.2 Bayesian Inference on a conjugate prior with p=2. Used as an benchmark. See Forbes & Mardia (2015) by \(\texttt{circglmbayes}\). For the von Mises distribution on \([0,2\pi)\), \(f(\theta\mid\mu,\kappa) = \frac{1}{2\pi I_0(\kappa)}\exp\{\kappa\cos(\theta-\mu)\}\), and conjugate prior \(\pi(\mu,\kappa) \propto I_0(\kappa)^{-c}\exp\{\kappa R_0\cos(\mu-\mu_0)\},\) with hyperparameters \(c\ge0\), \(R_0\ge0\), and \(\mu_0\). The resulting joint posterior is\[\pi(\mu,\kappa\mid\theta_{1:n}) \propto I_0(\kappa)^{-(c+n)}\exp{\kappa R_n\cos(\mu-\bar\theta_n)}\] where the posterior resultant length \(R_n\) and mean direction \(\bar\theta_n\) are defined by \(R_n\cos\bar\theta_n = R_0\cos\mu_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n}\cos\theta_i\) and \(R_n\sin\bar\theta_n = R_0\sin\mu_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sin\theta_i\). We sample from the posterior using a two‑step Gibbs sampler. Given current values \((\mu,\kappa)\), Draw \(\mu\mid\kappa,\theta\) from a von Mises distribution with mean direction \(\bar\theta_n\) and concentration \(\kappa R_n\). Draw \(\kappa\mid\mu,\theta\) from the full conditional \[ \pi(\kappa\mid\mu,\theta) \propto I_0(\kappa)^{-\eta}\exp\{-\eta\beta_0\kappa\}, \] where \(\eta = c+n\) and \(\beta_0 = -\frac{R_n}{\eta}\cos(\mu-\bar\theta_n)\). Here \(\mu \in [0,2\pi]\) as an angle. \(\kappa \geq0\) as concentration. Also, \(\texttt{theta}\) as an vector with each scalar \(\theta_i\in [0, 2\pi]\) and dimension n. Below we will show an brief example. Visualize Result after fitting data, 6.3 A simple example for MPS to help you draw graphies in the paper Though \(\texttt{bayesdir::run_predresamp_vmf()}\) in \(\texttt{bayesdir()}\) Visualize Result after fitting data, So to draw similar graphes with the paper, follow the comment below carefully 6.4 Comparison between MCMC and MPS Method. 7 OSCAR real-data application 7.1 Data and tuning 7.2 Helper functions 7.3 Load data and compute MLEs 7.4 Predictive resampling and post-correction 7.5 Post-corrected trace plot 7.6 Rose diagram and angular density

7 OSCAR real-data application This vignette reproduces the real-data results used in the OSCAR southern offshore compact application: the numerical summary table, the post-corrected trace-and-terminal-density figure, and the two-panel rose/density figure. The analysis uses the vMF predictive-resampling functions in our package; the small helper routines below only store full trajectories and produce the paper figures. 7.1 Data and tuning The two CSV files contains the preprocessed compact subarea observations for summer and winter, with columns y1 and y2 giving unit current-direction vectors after rotation to the common reference direction. For local testing, the code also searches the experiment folder used to prepare the paper figures. 7.2 Helper functions 7.3 Load data and compute MLEs 7.4 Predictive resampling and post-correction The chunk below reruns the full predictive-resampling experiment when rerun_mps = TRUE or when no cached result file is available. The full-path helper uses the same score, Fisher information, and affine variance correction as the exported bayesdir sampler, but stores the intermediate states needed for the trace figure. 7.5 Post-corrected trace plot 7.6 Rose diagram and angular density
